items.foco: Garden classification in femoral neck fractures
items.title: Garden classification
items.contentHtml:
Garden classification in femoral neck fractures

  • Garden I: incomplete or impacted valgus fracture (angled medial trabeculae <30°). Garden II: complete without scrolling. Garden III: complete with partial displacement (trabeculae still contact). Garden IV: complete with total displacement (trabeculae do not contact).
  • Prognostic threshold: types I-II are considered non-displaced (low risk of avascular necrosis); Types III-IV are displaced (high risk of avascular necrosis).
  • Snare in examination: Garden's classification is based on anteroposterior radiography; It does not evaluate rotational stability. To decide treatment, displacement (Garden III-IV) is the key factor.

items.foco: Blood supply to the femoral head
items.title: Femoral head irrigation
items.contentHtml:
Blood supply to the femoral head

  • The main supply comes from the medial and lateral circumflex arteries (branches of the deep femoral artery), which form the extracapsular arterial ring at the base of the neck.
  • The retinacular arteries (ascending) penetrate the capsule and supply the head; they are vulnerable in displaced fractures of the femoral neck.
  • The artery of the round ligament (branch of the obturator artery) only contributes to the fovea, insufficient for the entire head.
  • Questionable fact: in displaced intracapsular fractures, the interruption of the retinacular arteries is the main cause of avascular necrosis of the femoral head.

items.title: Intertrochanteric vs. subcapital
items.contentHtml:
Intertrochanteric fracture vs. subcapital

  • Subcapital fracture: intracapsular, interrupts retinacular irrigation; high risk of avascular necrosis and pseudoarthrosis. Garden classification.
  • Intertrochanteric fracture: extracapsular, good irrigation (spongy metaphysis); low risk of avascular necrosis, but greater bleeding and comorbidity.
  • Differential imaging diagnosis: the subcapital fracture line crosses the femoral neck proximal to the tuberosities; The intertrochanteric is located between the greater and lesser trochanters.
  • Trap: the intertrochanteric fracture can present with Pawels sign (fracture angle) but this applies to femoral neck fractures, not intertrochanteric.

items.foco: Treatment according to age and displacement
items.title: Treatment according to age and displacement
items.contentHtml:
Treatment according to age and displacement

  • Young patient (<65 years): displaced fracture (Garden III-IV) requires open reduction and internal fixation (cannulated screws) to preserve femoral head.
  • Elderly patient (>65 years) with displaced fracture: partial arthroplasty (hemiarthroplasty) or total hip arthroplasty depending on functional status and comorbidities.
  • Non-displaced fracture (Garden I-II): at any age, in situ internal fixation with cannulated screws or Dynamic Hip Screw (DHS).
  • First line in osteoporotic elderly with fracture intertrochanteric:intramedullary nail (Gamma type) for better biomechanical stability.

items.title: Indications for total arthroplasty
items.contentHtml:
Indications for total hip arthroplasty

  • Total hip arthroplasty is indicated in displaced femoral neck fractures when the patient has symptomatic preexisting osteoarthritis, inflammatory joint disease (rheumatoid arthritis), or previous avascular necrosis of the femoral head.
  • Also preferred in young active patients (<65 years) with displaced fracture and good functional demand, to avoid future revision of hemiarthroplasty.
  • In elderly people with high functional demand and life expectancy >10 years, total arthroplasty offers better functional results than hemiarthroplasty.
  • Relative contraindication: active infection or severe comorbidity that contraindicates surgery older.

items.foco: Complication: avascular necrosis of the femoral head
items.title: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
items.contentHtml:
Complication: avascular necrosis of the femoral head

  • Avascular necrosis of the femoral head is the most feared complication of displaced intracapsular fractures (Garden III-IV), with an incidence of 15-30%.
  • It occurs due to interruption of the flow of the retinacular arteries; irrigation from the round ligament artery is insufficient.
  • Clinically it manifests with pain in the groin and progressive functional limitation 6-24 months after fracture.
  • Diagnosis by magnetic resonance imaging (gold standard) or bone scintigraphy; A simple x-ray shows segmental collapse of the head (crescent sign).
  • Treatment: in early stages (pre-collapse) spinal decompression or vascularized graft; in collapse, total hip arthroplasty.

items.title: Pawels sign
items.contentHtml:
Pawels sign for fracture angle

  • Pawels sign: angle formed between the fracture line of the femoral neck and the horizontal (in anteroposterior radiograph). Classifies femoral neck fractures into three types.
  • Type I: angle <30° (horizontal fracture, stable, lower risk of displacement). Type II: angle 30-50° (intermediate risk). Type III: angle >50° (vertical fracture, unstable, high risk of nonunion and avascular necrosis).
  • The Pawels angle is used to decide fixation: types I-II can be managed with cannulated screws; type III requires Dynamic Hip Screw (DHS) or intramedullary nail for greater stability.
  • Trap: not to be confused with the Pauwels angle (same entity); Both terms are synonyms, but in exams it is usually written Pawels.

items.title: Evans classification
items.contentHtml:
Evans classification for intertrochanteric fractures

  • Evans classification divides intertrochanteric fractures into stable and unstable according to the integrity of the posteromedial cortex.
  • Type I: without posteromedial displacement (stable). Type II: posteromedial displacement (unstable). Type III: with fracture of the lesser trochanter (unstable). Type IV: with fracture of the greater trochanter (unstable). Type V: with subtrochanteric extension (unstable). of fixation in intertrochanteric fractures.

items.foco: Fixation with DHS or intramedullary nail
items.title: DHS vs. intramedullary nail
items.contentHtml:
Fixation with DHS or intramedullary nail

  • Dynamic Hip Screw (DHS): extramedullary device with sliding screw in the femoral neck and lateral plate. Indicated in stable intertrochanteric fractures (Evans I) and non-displaced femoral neck fractures.
  • Intramedullary nail (Gamma, PFNA): intramedullary device with cephalic screw. Preferred in unstable intertrochanteric fractures (Evans II-V) and subtrochanteric fractures, due to better biomechanics and less lever arm.
  • In displaced femoral neck fractures in young people, fixation with cannulated screws (3 in a triangle) is the first line, not DHS.
  • Trap: DHS is Contraindicated in fractures with severe posteromedial comminution (unstable) due to high risk of failure.

items.foco: Risk of nonunion in basic fractures
items.title: Pseudoarthrosis in basic fractures
items.contentHtml:
Risk of nonunion in basic fractures

  • Basic fractures of the femoral neck (fracture line at the base of the neck, near the intertrochanteric junction) have a risk of nonunion of 10-20% even with adequate fixation.
  • The risk increases in vertical fractures (Pawels angle >50°) and in patients with severe osteoporosis or smoking.
  • Pseudoarthrosis is defined as absence of radiological consolidation 6-9 months after fixation, with pain and mobility in the focus.
  • Treatment: revision with more rigid fixation (DHS or intramedullary nail) plus bone graft, or conversion to total hip arthroplasty in elderly.
  • Askable fact: the Pawels sign is the main radiographic predictor of nonunion in basic fractures.

items.foco: Management of fractures in osteoporotic elderly
items.title: Management in osteoporotic elderly
items.contentHtml:
Management of fractures in osteoporotic elderly people

  • In osteoporotic elderly people, hip fracture is a surgical emergency: surgery within the first 48 hours reduces mortality and complications (pneumonia, deep vein thrombosis, pressure ulcers).
  • Prefer partial arthroplasty (hemiarthroplasty) in displaced femoral neck fractures in patients >80 years or with low functional demand.
  • For intertrochanteric fractures, the intramedullary nail is superior to the Dynamic Hip Screw (DHS) due to a lower cut-out rate in osteoporotic bone.
  • Perioperative management: evaluate comorbidities (heart disease, diabetes), suspend anticoagulants, start antithrombotic prophylaxis with low molecular weight heparin.
  • Postoperative: supplementation with calcium and vitamin D, and treatment with bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) to prevent new fractures.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis with pelvic ramus fracture
items.title: Differential diagnosis with pelvic ramus fracture
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with pelvic ramus fracture

  • The pelvic ramus fracture (pubis, ischium) can simulate a hip fracture: pain in the groin and functional impotence for walking.
  • Key finding in exploration: in hip fracture, the pain is exacerbated by passive mobilization of the femoral neck (internal rotation of the foot); in pelvic ramus fracture, the pain is maximum with lateral compression of the iliac crests.
  • Plain radiography: the hip fracture is visualized in the anteroposterior projection of the pelvis and axial projection of the hip; pelvic ramus fracture is seen in the anteroposterior pelvis and inlet/outlet projection.
  • Trap: a pelvic ramus fracture can coexist with hip fracture (high energy injury); always evaluate both.
  • Computed tomography is useful when simple radiography is doubtful or to characterize the stroke.
